result:
At 5:17 a.m., the city is pretending not to be a city.

Streetlights are still on, but softer now, almost embarrassed by dawn. The bakery on the corner exhales its first warm breath through a side door that doesn’t fully close. Somewhere three blocks away, a bus kneels to the curb with a sigh that sounds almost human. If you stand still long enough, you can hear the rhythm under everything: compressors, footsteps, train tracks cooling, pigeons negotiating rooftop borders, distant highway like a sea with no shore.

This is my favorite hour to imagine that places are people.

The laundromat is a patient aunt in a bright cardigan, humming while she works. The glass tower downtown is an overachiever who hasn’t slept in twenty years and insists it’s fine, it’s totally fine. The old brick library with the cracked lion statues out front is an elderly professor who still writes letters by hand and carries peppermints in his coat pocket. The river is a teenager—restless, contradictory, reflective and opaque in alternating moods. Even the pothole outside my apartment has personality: cynical, opportunistic, and oddly loyal, returning every spring no matter what promises are made at city hall.

Maybe this is what we do to survive modern life: we animate the inanimate so we don’t feel so inanimate ourselves.

When I was younger, I thought adulthood would arrive all at once, like a certificate in the mail. Congratulations, you now understand taxes, your purpose, and where to buy a couch that won’t collapse under existential weather. Instead, adulthood turned out to be mostly recurring passwords and wondering what to make for dinner. It’s less coronation, more maintenance. Less trumpet fanfare, more replacing the air filter and trying not to spiral while comparing health insurance plans.

And yet there is something quietly heroic in maintenance.

A lot of our myths celebrate the singular act: slay the dragon, summit the mountain, win the war, publish the masterpiece. But ordinary life asks for repetition, not climax. Water the plants. Reply to the email. Sweep the floor. Apologize. Try again. Keep faith with small things. No one gives medals for doing the dishes five thousand times without becoming a nihilist, but maybe they should. Maybe endurance is just courage stretched across time.

I think often about the word “tend.”

It’s a humble word, practical and unglamorous. You tend a garden, a wound, a stove, a child, a grief. You tend to a task. You tend toward a habit. In astronomy, there is no “tend,” only massive laws and celestial mechanics. But here, on this gritty little planet where socks disappear in dryers and people cry in grocery store parking lots, tending is one of the highest arts.

To tend is to believe in a future you cannot guarantee.

Planting basil on a windowsill is absurdly optimistic. So is learning someone’s name and pronouncing it carefully. So is writing a novel, proposing marriage, adopting a dog, beginning physical therapy, buying postcards you may never send. Hope is not always a blazing bonfire. Sometimes it is a pilot light: tiny, blue, stubborn.

Of course, hope has competitors.

There is cynicism, which feels intelligent for about seven minutes and then becomes a kind of malnutrition. There is distraction, infinitely available and frictionless. There is irony, useful in small doses and corrosive in bulk. There is despair, which can masquerade as realism because it asks so little of us. If nothing matters, we’re off the hook. No risk, no disappointment. But also no surprise, no intimacy, no transformation.

I don’t think meaning arrives like lightning. I think meaning accumulates like lint: quietly, daily, from contact.

You call your friend back.
You learn the barista’s schedule.
You return the shopping cart.
You read the poem again, slower.
You forgive someone before they become easy to forgive.
You forgive yourself before you become easy to forgive.

And one day you look around and realize your life is not a puzzle you solved but a fabric you wove.

There are dropped stitches, sure. Burn marks, uneven edges, colors that clash in daylight. But it holds.

Lately I’ve been interested in thresholds—doorways, train platforms, the split second between notification and response, the inhale before speaking. We spend so much time there, suspended between versions of ourselves. Not who we were, not yet who we might be. Culture likes before-and-after stories because they’re clean. Real life is mostly during.

During is where we hesitate.
During is where we practice.
During is where we misread the map and discover a better street.
During is where identity ferments.

A friend once told me she felt like a draft of a person. I knew what she meant. We all have that feeling: unfinished, marked up, awkward in the margins. But maybe “draft” is not an insult. Drafts are alive. They can change. Stone tablets cannot.

The trouble with perfection is that it cannot be touched up. The beauty of being a draft is that revision is possible.

Revision, not reinvention. I don’t trust total reinvention stories anymore. They often require amnesia or denial, and the past has a way of mailing itself forward. Revision is gentler and more truthful. Keep the spine, edit the sentences. Clarify the thesis. Remove what was performative. Strengthen what was honest. Add evidence. Cite your sources: joy, fatigue, ancestry, accident, weather, luck, labor, love.

Love especially.

Not just romance—the algorithmically marketable kind with sunsets and matching mugs—but the broader, stranger force. The love that packs oranges for a night shift. The love that says “text me when you get home” and means it. The love that sits in hospital chairs and learns new vocabulary overnight. The love that leaves voicemail rambles. The love that asks better questions. The love that holds boundaries like bridges, not walls.

If I sound sentimental, fine. Sentimentality is sincerity without structure. I’m aiming for sincerity with structure, like a well-built porch where people can sit and tell the truth.

Truth, for instance: I am often afraid.

Afraid of wasting time, making the wrong choice, saying the wrong thing, becoming irrelevant, becoming rigid, losing people, losing nerve. Afraid of the scale of suffering in the world and the smallness of my reach. Afraid that attention itself has become a mined landscape and I keep stepping in the wrong places.

But fear is data, not destiny.

Fear tells you where you’re invested. It points to your attachments, your unfinished business, your tenderness. It can become a compass if you stop treating it like a verdict. The question is not “How do I eliminate fear?” but “What do I want to protect, and what am I willing to build despite uncertainty?”

Build is another good word.

Build a reading habit.
Build a neighborhood ritual.
Build a savings cushion.
Build a compost pile.
Build a language between generations.
Build trust slowly enough that it lasts weather.

We are always building something, even when we think we’re standing still. Neglect builds too—mold in corners, silence between siblings, policies that calcify into suffering. Entropy is productive. If we don’t choose our construction projects, default settings choose for us.

So choose.

Choose deliberately, if not dramatically. Choose what kind of attention you want to practice. Choose where to place your one wild and ordinary life. Choose a few values sturdy enough to survive your moods. Choose people who can tell you the truth without making you feel disposable. Choose rest before collapse chooses it for you.

And while you’re at it, choose delight with less apology.

Delight is not frivolous. It is fuel.
The perfect peach in July.
The dog that insists on carrying a stick three times its size.
A song that finds the exact shape of your ache.
The smell of rain on hot pavement.
A clean notebook.
A train arriving exactly when you reach the platform, as if the universe briefly remembered your name.

These are not distractions from life. They are life.

At 6:02 a.m., the city gives up pretending. Shutters rattle open. Coffee grinders roar. Delivery trucks reverse with shrill beeps. Someone laughs too loudly on a phone call. Someone cries behind sunglasses. Someone proposes. Someone resigns. Someone decides to stay. Someone decides to go.

The day begins, as days do, without asking whether we feel ready.

That may be the most merciful thing about time: it moves. It carries us past old scenes we thought we’d live in forever. It also carries us into responsibilities we didn’t ask for and joys we didn’t foresee. We cannot hold it still, but we can meet it with a certain style—with attention, with humor, with a willingness to be changed.

If I have a philosophy, maybe it is this:

Be porous but not shapeless.
Be kind but not indefinite.
Be ambitious but not extractive.
Be skeptical but not sterile.
Be devoted to something larger than your reflection.
Tend what is in reach.
Mourn what is lost.
Repair what you can.
Learn names.
Keep promises.
Make soup.
Read good sentences out loud.
Look up when birds pass overhead, even if you can’t identify them.
And when the hour is early and the city is pretending not to be a city, walk through it as if you belong here—because you do.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
